channel.title: News Articles on IQSS Clone - Harvard Institute for Quantitative Social Science
channel.description: Recent content in News Articles on IQSS Clone - Harvard Institute for Quantitative Social Science

item.title: 2023 OpenDP Community Meeting Recap
item.description:
The 2023 OpenDP Community Meeting was held on September 29th at Harvard University, bringing together researchers, practitioners, and developers focused on differential privacy. The event was organized in conjunction with the TPDP (Theory and Practice of Differential Privacy) workshop, marking the first in-person gathering for the OpenDP community since the project’s inception.

Attendees explored the latest developments in the OpenDP Library, discussed real-world deployment challenges, and shared insights on building privacy-preserving data analysis systems. Presentations covered topics ranging from new algorithmic implementations to user experience improvements in the DP Creator application.

item.title: 2024 OpenDP Community Meeting - Recap and Survey
item.description:
The 2024 OpenDP Community Meeting was held August 22-23 at Harvard University, once again joining forces with TPDP (Theory and Practice of Differential Privacy). The two-day event brought together differential privacy researchers, software developers, and data practitioners from academia, government, and industry.

Key highlights included demonstrations of new OpenDP Library features, panel discussions on differential privacy deployment challenges, and breakout sessions on topics such as privacy budget management and user-facing tools. Participants also engaged in collaborative problem-solving around real-world privacy scenarios.

item.title: A focus on flourishing: Newest IQSS program
item.description:
The Human Flourishing Program, IQSS’s newest scientific program, combines social science and philosophy to understand the factors that lead to a good life. Directed by Tyler VanderWeele, the program integrates knowledge from empirical research and the humanities on topics including religion, virtue, work, purpose, forgiveness, marriage, and family.

The program’s interdisciplinary approach sets it apart from traditional academic research centers. By bringing together researchers from psychology, epidemiology, philosophy, and theology, the Human Flourishing Program examines well-being through multiple lenses simultaneously, producing insights that no single discipline could achieve alone.

item.title: A Love of Learning and Helping Others Succeed: Wendy Guan Retires
item.description:
By Colleen Walsh

A map covered in red dots hangs on Wendy Guan’s office wall. The dense rosy points obscure what lies beneath until a closer look reveals Italy, Sicily, and the tip of Tunisia.

Those vibrant points enabled Harvard’s Francis Goelet Professor of Medieval History Michael McCormick to connect the dots. He had turned to Guan and her team at Harvard’s IQSS Center for Geographic Analysis (CGA) to help track the path of the Plague of Justinian (541–ca. 750 CE) for his medieval history class.

item.title: A New Industry-Academic Initiative at IQSS
item.description:
IQSS has announced a new initiative designed to transform academic social science research into market-ready startup ventures. The new industry-academic connections being built will help social scientists increase scholarship impact, harvest data and resources from the commercial world for research, and enable universities and businesses to learn from each other more effectively.

As part of this effort, IQSS welcomes the “Startup Foundry” (with a new name to be chosen soon), which has been moved from its previous home at Harvard Business School. The initiative is led by its director and newest IQSS team member, Debi Kleiman, and is supported by contributors Kelly and Carla Kimball.

item.title: Affiliate Spotlight: Adam Stefkovics
item.description:
Adam Stefkovics is a visiting researcher at IQSS with a Fulbright scholarship, an assistant lecturer at Eotvos Lorand University in Budapest, and director of polling at the Szazadveg Foundation. His research focuses on survey methodology, political polling, and public opinion analysis in Central European democracies.

During his time at IQSS, Stefkovics has been working on projects related to survey design and measurement, benefiting from the institute’s expertise in quantitative methods and access to collaborative research resources. His work bridges Hungarian and American academic traditions in political science and survey research.

item.title: Affiliate Spotlight: Jacob Brown
item.description:
Jacob is a resident graduate student at IQSS who is graduating this spring with a PhD in Government and Social Policy. If you see him around CGIS, be sure to wish him congratulations and good luck!

What is the focus of your work?

“Political geography and political behavior in American politics.”

What’s something you like about IQSS?

“The grad students have semi-regular dinners that are a lot of fun.”

item.title: Affiliate Spotlight: Maria Ballesteros
item.description:
Maria Ballesteros is a graduate student and resident affiliate at IQSS, pursuing her PhD in the Department of Government at Harvard. Her research focuses on political behavior, institutions, and methodology, with particular attention to how political systems shape citizen engagement and representation.

As a GSAS Prize Fellow, Ballesteros brings exceptional academic credentials to her work at IQSS, where she benefits from the institute’s collaborative environment, research computing resources, and community of quantitative social scientists. She regularly participates in IQSS seminars and workshops.

item.title: Affiliate Spotlight: Sascha Riaz
item.description:
Sascha Riaz is a Harvard Government PhD student (G5) and resident affiliate at IQSS. His research examines political economy, with a focus on how historical institutions shape contemporary political and economic outcomes, particularly in South Asian contexts.

As a resident affiliate, Riaz has been an active member of the IQSS community, participating in seminars, collaborating with other researchers, and leveraging the institute’s data science resources for his dissertation work. His methodological approach combines quantitative analysis with historical case studies.

item.title: AI leaps from math dunce to whiz
item.description:
IQSS affiliate Michael Brenner, the Michael F. Cronin Professor of Applied Mathematics and Applied Physics, explains how recent AI advancements have opened new possibilities for mathematical application. What was once considered a fundamental limitation of artificial intelligence—its inability to perform complex mathematical reasoning—has been dramatically overcome by newer model architectures.

Brenner discusses how these advances enable researchers to tackle problems that were previously computationally intractable, from solving differential equations to discovering new mathematical theorems. The implications extend across scientific disciplines where mathematical modeling plays a central role.

item.title: Al Roth Receives Nobel Prize in Economics
item.description:
IQSS affiliate Al Roth (Harvard Business School) and Lloyd S. Shapley were awarded the 2012 Sveriges Riksbank Prize in Economic Sciences in Memory of Alfred Nobel for their work on the theory of stable allocations and the practice of market design. The prize recognized how their research had moved from abstract theory to practical applications that improve people’s lives.

Roth’s work on market design has led to better systems for matching kidney donors with patients, assigning students to schools, and placing medical residents in hospitals. His approach combines game theory with careful empirical observation to design mechanisms that produce stable, efficient outcomes in markets where prices alone cannot do the job.

item.title: An Amazing 2025 Dataverse Community Meeting
item.description:
The IQSS Dataverse Core team recently returned from the 2025 Dataverse Community Meeting, which was hosted by the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. The event represented the culmination of two and a half years of intensive development work, resulting in several major milestones for the Dataverse Project.

Key Highlights

  • A new React-based frontend, expected to be available in production in July 2025
  • The launch of Dataverse Hub and Dataverse Marketplace
  • Significant advancements in AI readiness, including full support for the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Croissant-ML metadata schema. Dataverse was selected as one of the data repositories for the NeurIPS25 conference, described as “the most important AI conference of the year”
  • AutoSage — an AI Metadata helper built on open source large language models, fully trained at IQSS on the complete Harvard Dataverse data, along with the forthcoming Dataverse Knowledge Graph
  • A powerful External Search Tool that uses natural language queries to interrogate the Dataverse repository alongside traditional keyword and facet-based search
  • New integration with high performance computing infrastructures, including the internal FAS-RC cluster, via a tool called LOOP that connects OpenOnDemand and Dataverse seamlessly (a collaboration between the Dataverse and HPC teams at IQSS)
  • New Large Data Support services deployed at scale

Community Growth

The Dataverse community continues to expand, with worldwide installations growing from 70 in 2022 to over 135 today. The meeting showcased contributions from institutions and developers across the globe.

item.title: Announcing OpenDP Library 0.5
item.description:
The OpenDP Project is excited to announce the release of OpenDP Library 0.5, introducing significant architectural improvements that provide a more flexible and powerful approach to tracking privacy budgets and composing differentially private algorithms. This release represents a major step forward in making differential privacy more accessible and practical for real-world data analysis workflows.

Version 0.5 introduces enhanced mechanisms for privacy accounting, allowing developers to build more sophisticated differentially private systems with greater precision and control. The flexible privacy tracking framework makes it easier to compose complex analyses while maintaining rigorous mathematical guarantees about privacy loss. These improvements enable data practitioners to extract more utility from their protected datasets while still ensuring strong privacy protections for individuals represented in the data.

item.title: Announcing OpenDP Library 0.8
item.description:
The OpenDP team announced the release of OpenDP Library 0.8, a modular collection of algorithms for building privacy-preserving applications. This version focuses on expanding the library’s capabilities while maintaining its commitment to mathematical rigor and verified privacy guarantees.

Version 0.8 introduces new transformations and measurements, improved documentation, and enhanced interoperability with common data science workflows. The release reflects ongoing collaboration between Harvard’s OpenDP team and the broader differential privacy research community.

item.title: Announcing OpenDP Library 0.9
item.description:
The OpenDP Project is proud to announce the release of OpenDP Library 0.9, a major milestone that expands the modular collection of differentially private algorithms available for building robust privacy-preserving applications. This release brings powerful new capabilities to developers and data scientists who need to extract insights from sensitive data while providing mathematically rigorous privacy guarantees.

Version 0.9 significantly expands the library’s algorithmic toolkit, adding new measurements, transformations, and composition mechanisms that enable more sophisticated privacy-preserving analyses. The modular architecture allows users to construct custom differentially private pipelines by combining building blocks in flexible ways, each with formal privacy proofs that ensure the overall system maintains its privacy guarantees. Enhanced support for common data analysis tasks makes it easier to adopt differential privacy without requiring deep expertise in the underlying mathematics.

item.title: Announcing the 2021 IQSS Graduate Research Grant Recipients
item.description:
The Institute for Quantitative Social Science is pleased to announce the recipients of the 2021 IQSS Graduate Research Grants, a competitive program that provides crucial financial support for dissertation research by graduate students across Harvard’s social science departments. These grants enable students to conduct ambitious research projects that require funding for data collection, fieldwork, specialized software, research assistance, or other resources essential to completing high-quality dissertation work.

The IQSS Graduate Research Grant program reflects the institute’s commitment to supporting the next generation of quantitative social scientists by removing financial barriers to innovative research. Recipients represent diverse disciplines and methodological approaches, from experimental and survey research to computational text analysis and spatial studies. The grants provide students with flexibility to pursue their research visions while benefiting from IQSS’s broader ecosystem of methodological expertise, consulting services, and collaborative community.

item.title: Announcing the 2023 OpenDP Community Meeting
item.description:
The OpenDP Project is thrilled to announce the first in-person OpenDP Community Meeting, bringing together researchers, developers, and practitioners working at the intersection of data privacy, statistical analysis, and open-source software development. This historic gathering marks an important milestone for the OpenDP ecosystem, providing a venue for face-to-face collaboration, knowledge sharing, and community building around practical differential privacy tools.

The meeting will feature presentations on the latest developments in the OpenDP Library, case studies from organizations deploying differential privacy in production systems, and technical workshops that help participants gain hands-on experience with privacy-preserving data analysis. Sessions will cover topics ranging from fundamental concepts for newcomers to advanced techniques for experienced practitioners, ensuring valuable content for attendees at all levels. The gathering also provides crucial opportunities for informal discussions, networking, and collaborative problem-solving that are difficult to replicate in virtual settings.

item.title: Announcing the Magaro Peer Pre-Review Program
item.description: IQSS is proud to announce the launch of the Alexander and Diviya Magaro Peer Pre-Review Program, an innovative initiative designed to improve scholarly research quality and accelerate publication success by providing authors with rigorous peer feedback before journal submission. This program addresses a common challenge in academic publishing: manuscripts often receive first peer feedback many months after journal submission, leading to lengthy revision cycles that slow the advancement of knowledge.

item.title: Beyond 100 Installations: Dataverse's Growth and Global Role in Research Data Sharing
item.description:
The Dataverse Project, developed at IQSS, has surpassed 100 installations worldwide, marking a significant milestone in its mission to make research data sharing accessible to scholars globally. This growth reflects the platform’s adoption across universities, research institutions, and government agencies on every continent.

From its origins as the Harvard Dataverse Network, the project has evolved into a truly global infrastructure for research data management. Each installation represents an independent repository where researchers can deposit, share, and preserve their data according to FAIR principles—making it Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable.

item.title: Bob Treacy: From the Factory Floor to Harvard and 30 Years of Java
item.description: Bob Treacy, Principal Software Architect on the Dataverse Project at IQSS, recently shared his remarkable journey from working on factory floors to becoming a key architect of one of the world’s leading open-source research data repository platforms. His story offers unique insights into the evolution of software development over three decades and the enduring relevance of Java, the programming language that has been central to his career and to the Dataverse platform.

item.title: Building terraFlow: How an Idea in the Lab Grew Into a Venture-Backed Startup
item.description: Dan Freeman, a Harvard PhD student in Biology and Biomedical Sciences, had prior experience with single-cell technology and pharma trials. Convinced there was a better approach, he began experimenting with artificial intelligence and arrived at his breakthrough concept. His vision involved using AI to accelerate drug discovery, and he had developed an early prototype. What he needed was guidance on commercialization—identifying customers, refining his pitch, and building a business plan. The IQSS Startup Foundry provided that support.

item.title: Call for Papers: 2026 CGA Conference
item.description: The Center for Geographic Analysis has issued a call for paper submissions for its 2026 conference, marking a special milestone as the center celebrates its 20th anniversary. The conference theme—past, present, and future—invites researchers to reflect on two decades of innovation in geographic analysis while exploring emerging frontiers in geospatial science and technology. Submissions are welcome from across all disciplines that use geographic analysis, spatial data, and mapping technologies in their research.

item.title: Call for Proposals: Dataverse Community Meeting 2025
item.description:
The Dataverse Project is excited to announce a call for proposals for the 2025 Dataverse Community Meeting, which will be hosted at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. This annual gathering is the premier event for the global Dataverse community, and the program committee invites submissions from repository administrators, developers, researchers, data professionals, and anyone working with or interested in the Dataverse platform.

Proposals are welcome across a wide range of topics related to research data management, open science infrastructure, and the Dataverse ecosystem. Potential topics include case studies of Dataverse implementations, technical presentations on new features or integrations, discussions of data curation workflows, explorations of policy and governance challenges, and demonstrations of tools or extensions. Both individual presentations and panel sessions are encouraged, with formats ranging from traditional talks to interactive workshops and hands-on tutorials.

item.title: CGA creates Congressional District COVID-19 Dashboard
item.description: The Center for Geographic Analysis has created a comprehensive public dashboard that tracks COVID-19 data by congressional district, providing policymakers, researchers, and the public with geographically granular information about the pandemic’s spread and impact across the United States. This innovative tool fills a critical gap in COVID-19 data visualization by organizing information at the congressional district level, enabling more effective policy responses and helping lawmakers understand how the pandemic affects their constituents.

item.title: CGA develops RINX: Open-Source Solution for Big Raster Datasets
item.description: The Center for Geographic Analysis has developed RINX (Raster Index), an innovative open-source solution designed to address critical challenges in processing and analyzing big raster datasets from Earth observation systems. As satellite imagery and remote sensing data grow exponentially in volume and resolution, researchers face increasing technical barriers to working with these valuable resources. RINX provides an efficient indexing and retrieval system that makes large-scale raster analysis more accessible to the geospatial science community.

item.title: CGA Geographic Insights Lab Launches Initiative with Indian Government
item.description: The CGA Geographic Insights Lab has launched a significant initiative with the Government of India, with India Policy Insights being designated as a Key Initiative of NITI Aayog, the premier policy think tank of the Indian government. This partnership represents a major collaboration between Harvard researchers and Indian policymakers to leverage geospatial data and analysis for evidence-based governance and development planning across one of the world’s most populous and diverse nations.

item.title: CGA Project Selected for NSF Challenge
item.description: A Center for Geographic Analysis project focused on mapping geographical locations for successful market entry has been selected as a winner of the NSF I-GUIDE Spatial AI Challenge. This recognition highlights the innovative application of spatial artificial intelligence and geographic analysis techniques to solve real-world business and economic development challenges. The project demonstrates how advanced geospatial methods can provide actionable insights for organizations seeking to optimize their location decisions.

item.title: CGA releases Twitter Sentiment Geographical Index
item.description:
The Center for Geographic Analysis has released the Twitter Sentiment Geographical Index, an innovative tool that derives well-being indicators from social media data to track emotional patterns and public sentiment across geographic regions. This project demonstrates how large-scale analysis of publicly available social media posts can complement traditional surveys and administrative data to provide more timely, granular insights into population well-being and how it varies across space.

The Twitter Sentiment Geographical Index employs natural language processing and sentiment analysis techniques to categorize the emotional content of tweets, then aggregates these sentiment scores at various geographic scales from neighborhoods to cities to regions. By analyzing millions of social media posts over time, researchers can identify patterns in population mood, track responses to events or policies, and detect geographic variations in subjective well-being that might not be captured by conventional data sources. The tool provides a near-real-time window into collective emotional states across communities.

item.title: CGA Welcomes New Executive Director Dr. Rachel Franklin
item.description: Starting March 3, 2025, geographer Dr. Rachel Franklin will join the Center for Geographic Analysis (CGA), part of the Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS) at Harvard University, as Executive Director. Franklin succeeds Dr. Wendy Guan and will lead all operations of Harvard’s largest organization focused on geographic research, technology, and teaching, collaborating with Professor S.V. Subramanian, chair of the CGA Faculty Advisory Group. The CGA provides consultation services, technical training, platform development, and sponsored research to support geospatial analysis and GIS projects across the University.

item.title: CGA: Geospatial Infrastructure Enhancement
item.description:
The Center for Geographic Analysis has undertaken a major geospatial infrastructure enhancement project to address growing demand for robust, scalable systems capable of handling geospatial big data. As researchers across disciplines increasingly work with massive spatial datasets—from high-resolution satellite imagery to detailed geographic information about billions of people and places—the technical infrastructure supporting this work must evolve to keep pace with data volume, velocity, and complexity.

The infrastructure enhancement encompasses multiple dimensions including expanded storage capacity, high-performance computing resources optimized for spatial operations, improved data management systems, and enhanced tools for large-scale geoprocessing. These improvements enable researchers to tackle questions that would be computationally infeasible with traditional GIS infrastructure, such as analyzing decades of daily satellite imagery for the entire planet or modeling complex spatial-temporal phenomena at fine geographic scales. The enhanced infrastructure supports both individual research projects and shared services that benefit the broader Harvard research community.

item.title: Class of 2026 at IQSS: Whats Next?
item.description:
The IQSS community includes affiliated graduate students across Harvard University engaged in social science research. As the academic year ends, some graduate students complete their programs and move on. Below are updates from graduating affiliates continuing their work beyond Harvard.

Dorothy Manevich (PhD, Government, American Politics)

Dorothy is joining the national political department of the United Autoworkers Union (UAW).

Jeremiah Cha (PhD, Government)

In the fall, Jeremiah will join the Center for the Study of Democratic Politics at Princeton University.

item.title: Community Announcement: New Space Arrangements at IQSS
item.description:
IQSS is pleased to announce new space arrangements in the CGIS Knafel Building designed to better serve the research community as we adapt to post-pandemic work patterns and embrace more flexible approaches to office space utilization. These changes reflect lessons learned during the pandemic about how researchers work most effectively and acknowledge the evolving needs of our community as members balance in-person collaboration with remote work flexibility.

The new arrangements include a hybrid approach to office space that accommodates both dedicated workspace for staff and programs while providing bookable desk space for affiliated researchers who need on-campus work areas on a flexible basis. This system enables more efficient use of IQSS facilities while ensuring that community members have access to professional workspace when they need it. The changes also support collaboration by creating more shared spaces where researchers can connect with colleagues, access specialized resources, and participate in the intellectual life of the institute.

item.title: Crime and Police Reform In America—Does Politics Matter?
item.description:
by Courtney Hayes

On May 25, 2020, a Minneapolis police officer killed George Floyd by kneeling on his neck for almost 10 minutes. Floyd’s death sparked the largest mass protest movement in U.S. history and a national reckoning on race and police reform.

In the fall of 2020, pressure to act was intensifying. Protestors called for radical reform while crime rates spiked. Between 2019 and 2020, the U.S. murder rate rose 30%—the largest single-year increase in more than a century. As election season approached, former President Donald Trump began blaming Democratic mayors.

item.title: Crunching Data on American Elections: The CCES
item.description:
Since 2006, Stephen Ansolabehere has been compiling data about who votes in America and why as principal investigator for the Cooperative Congressional Election Study (CCES, now the Cooperative Election Study). Supported by IQSS, the study has become one of the most important resources for understanding American electoral behavior.

The CCES surveys tens of thousands of Americans before and after each election, gathering detailed information about their political attitudes, demographic characteristics, and voting behavior. This massive sample size enables researchers to analyze political dynamics at the state and congressional district level—something impossible with smaller surveys.

item.title: Cynthia Dwork wins Knuth Prize
item.description:
IQSS faculty affiliate Cynthia Dwork, Gordon McKay Professor of Computer Science, received the 2020 Donald E. Knuth Prize from SIGACT for her impactful work in the foundations of computer science. The prize recognizes sustained excellence in theoretical computer science research over an entire career.

Dwork is best known as the inventor of differential privacy, a mathematical framework that enables statistical analysis of databases while provably protecting individual privacy. This work has had enormous practical impact, with differential privacy now deployed by the U.S. Census Bureau, Apple, Google, and other major organizations.

item.title: Data Highway: A Trailblazing Journey into Trucking
item.description:
by Courtney Hayes

As highways become a new digital landscape where every truck serves as a node and every delivery a connection, a Harvard PhD-turned-entrepreneur is helping build tomorrow’s transportation infrastructure. IQSS affiliate Jinyan Zang, founder and COO of Axle, has created a data platform that seeks to transform trucking logistics.

The trucking industry today is massive. With almost four million tractor-trailers on US highways, trucking accounts for over 70% of the nation’s freight. The internet now connects every truck in the United States with the potential to track fuel levels, speed, location, and more. But the industry is highly fragmented, with thousands of brokers and dispatchers working around the clock to manage siloed data streams. As a result, up to 30% of all vehicles are driving empty. Zang notes: “That’s burning a ton of diesel without actually doing any work. That’s money out of the driver’s and shipper’s pocket.”

item.title: Data Science Services at IQSS: Harnessing Data Interpretation for Academic Advancement
item.description:
IQSS Data Science Services (DSS) provides research support to the Harvard community through consulting, collaboration, and analytical tool building. The team of PhD data science specialists assists researchers at every stage of the research process, from study design through publication.

The article profiles how academic research cycles follow systematic stages—from formulating research questions through data collection, analysis, and dissemination—and how DSS provides tailored support at each phase. Whether researchers need short-term advice on statistical methods or long-term collaborative partnerships, DSS adapts its services accordingly.

item.title: Data Science Students Visit FTC with Latanya Sweeney
item.description:
Professor Latanya Sweeney traveled with IQSS staff to share her students’ work with the U.S. Federal Trade Commission. As director of the Data Privacy Lab at IQSS, Sweeney has long focused on the intersection of technology, privacy, and public policy.

The visit gave students a rare opportunity to present their research findings directly to federal regulators responsible for protecting consumers from unfair or deceptive practices in the digital marketplace. Projects presented addressed real-world privacy vulnerabilities in commercial technologies and proposed evidence-based policy solutions.

item.title: DataFest 2020 Gathers Researchers
item.description:
DataFest 2020 brought together researchers from across Harvard and beyond for an intensive two-day series of workshops and presentations focused on analyzing, visualizing, and managing data in the modern research environment. This annual event, organized by IQSS, provides graduate students, postdoctoral researchers, and faculty with concentrated exposure to cutting-edge data science methods, tools, and best practices that enhance their research capabilities.

The 2020 program featured sessions covering the full lifecycle of data-intensive research, from data collection and cleaning through exploratory analysis, statistical modeling, visualization, and reproducible research workflows. Expert instructors demonstrated contemporary tools and techniques including programming in R and Python, version control with Git, effective data visualization principles, and approaches to managing complex datasets. The intensive format allowed participants to gain hands-on experience with new methods while connecting with peers facing similar methodological challenges across diverse research domains.

item.title: Dataverse 6.0 has arrived!
item.description:
The Dataverse Project is excited to announce the arrival of version 6.0, a major milestone release that brings comprehensive platform upgrades to ensure the software runs on modern, well-supported infrastructure components. This significant update includes upgrades to Payara application server, Solr search engine, and Java runtime environment, positioning Dataverse repositories on a solid foundation for continued growth and development.

The infrastructure upgrades in version 6.0 provide critical improvements to security, performance, and long-term maintainability. Moving to current versions of Payara, Solr, and Java ensures that Dataverse installations benefit from the latest bug fixes, security patches, and performance optimizations available in these core technologies. These platform updates also enable future feature development by providing access to modern capabilities and APIs that weren’t available in older versions. While platform upgrades require careful planning and testing, they are essential for maintaining a healthy, sustainable software ecosystem.

item.title: Dataverse 6.1 is Here
item.description:
The Dataverse Project is pleased to announce the release of version 6.1, the first feature release following the major 6.0 platform upgrade. This release showcases the strength of community-driven development, with significant contributions from institutions and developers across the global Dataverse network who are collectively shaping the future of open-source research data infrastructure.

Version 6.1 builds on the solid foundation established in 6.0, introducing new features and enhancements that improve functionality for researchers and repository administrators. Community contributions in this release span a wide range of improvements, from user interface refinements and workflow optimizations to new API capabilities and administrative tools. These collaborative efforts demonstrate how the open-source model enables institutions to pool expertise and resources, creating solutions that benefit the entire ecosystem rather than individual repositories in isolation.

item.title: Dataverse 6.2 is now available
item.description:
The Dataverse Project is excited to announce that version 6.2 is now available, bringing a valuable collection of updates and new features that enhance the platform’s capabilities for managing and sharing research data. This release continues the steady evolution of the 6.x series, delivering improvements that respond to community feedback and emerging needs in the research data management landscape.

Version 6.2 introduces features that improve both the researcher experience and administrative functionality. Enhanced data management tools make it easier to organize collections and apply appropriate access controls, while improvements to metadata handling support more comprehensive documentation of research outputs. New capabilities for data visualization and preview help users understand dataset contents more effectively, promoting data reuse and discovery. Administrative enhancements provide repository managers with better tools for monitoring system health, managing user permissions, and configuring repository behavior to meet institutional requirements.

item.title: Dataverse 6.5 is here!
item.description:
The Dataverse Project is pleased to announce the release of version 6.5, bringing a robust set of new features, enhancements, and bug fixes to the world’s most widely adopted open-source research data repository platform. This release reflects months of collaborative development by contributors from institutions around the globe, continuing Dataverse’s evolution as an essential infrastructure for research data management and sharing.

Version 6.5 introduces several user-requested features that improve both the researcher and administrator experience. New capabilities enhance metadata management, search functionality, and data visualization tools, making it easier for researchers to discover, access, and understand datasets. Performance optimizations ensure that repositories can handle growing collections efficiently, while security enhancements protect sensitive research data. The release also includes improvements to external tool integrations, expanding the ways researchers can analyze and work with their data directly from the Dataverse interface.

item.title: Dataverse Joins NIH in Increasing Access to Biomedical Data
item.description: The Dataverse Project has joined a major National Institutes of Health data curation initiative aimed at increasing access to biomedical data and supporting the NIH’s commitment to open science and data sharing. This partnership positions Dataverse as a key infrastructure component in the NIH’s strategy to ensure that federally funded biomedical research data is preserved, accessible, and reusable by the scientific community, accelerating the pace of discovery and maximizing the return on research investment.

item.title: Dataverse Publishes Houghton Library SAEF Data Collection
item.description: The Harvard Dataverse has published a significant new collection from Houghton Library: the Slavery, Abolition, Emancipation, and Freedom (SAEF) Data Collection, making valuable historical research data freely available to scholars worldwide. This collection brings together data related to one of the most consequential periods in American history, documenting the experiences of enslaved people, the abolition movement, emancipation processes, and the struggle for freedom and civil rights in the nineteenth century.

item.title: Dataverse Software 5.11.1 Release
item.description:
The Dataverse Project has announced the release of version 5.11.1, a maintenance update focused on addressing critical bugs and improving system stability. This patch release is part of the Dataverse team’s commitment to providing reliable, production-ready software for research data repositories around the world.

Version 5.11.1 includes important fixes that enhance the overall performance and user experience of the platform. These improvements address issues reported by the global Dataverse community, ensuring that repositories can continue to operate smoothly and researchers can access and share their data without interruption. The release demonstrates the responsive nature of the open-source development process, where community feedback directly shapes software improvements.

item.title: Dataverse Software 5.12 Release
item.description:
The Dataverse Project is pleased to announce the release of version 5.12, delivering a comprehensive set of new features, enhancements, and bug fixes that improve the research data repository experience for both users and administrators. This release continues the project’s commitment to regular, high-quality updates that respond to the needs of the global Dataverse community.

Version 5.12 introduces several highly requested features that enhance data discovery, access management, and metadata handling. Improvements to the user interface make common workflows more intuitive, while new administrative tools give repository managers better control over their collections and improved insight into repository usage. Enhanced API capabilities expand integration possibilities with other research tools and institutional systems, supporting more seamless data management workflows across the research lifecycle.

item.title: Dataverse Software 5.14 Release
item.description:
The Dataverse Project announces the release of version 5.14, bringing an extensive collection of new features, enhancements, and bug fixes that strengthen the platform’s capabilities as a comprehensive research data repository solution. This release reflects ongoing collaboration among developers, repository administrators, and researchers worldwide who contribute to making Dataverse more powerful and user-friendly.

Version 5.14 introduces meaningful improvements across multiple areas of the platform. Enhanced metadata management features provide researchers with more flexible ways to describe their datasets, improving discoverability and promoting best practices in data documentation. New visualization and preview capabilities make it easier for users to understand dataset contents before downloading, while improved access controls give data owners more granular options for managing who can view and use their research outputs. API enhancements expand programmatic access to repository functions, supporting automation and integration with institutional research infrastructure.

item.title: Dataverse Software 6.10 Release
item.description:
The Dataverse Project has announced the release of version 6.10 of the Dataverse software, bringing significant improvements to the world’s most widely adopted open-source research data repository platform. This latest release includes enhanced export capabilities, new application programming interfaces (APIs), infrastructure and performance updates, and numerous bug fixes that improve stability and user experience for repositories worldwide.

The export improvements in version 6.10 make it easier for researchers to extract their data and metadata in various formats, supporting interoperability with other research tools and platforms. New APIs expand the programmatic capabilities available to developers and repository administrators, enabling more sophisticated integrations and automation workflows. Infrastructure and performance enhancements ensure that Dataverse continues to scale effectively as datasets grow larger and repository usage increases.

item.title: Dataverse Software 6.4 Release
item.description:
The Dataverse Project is pleased to announce the release of version 6.4, continuing the momentum of the 6.x series with important bug fixes and new features that enhance the platform’s functionality and reliability. This release demonstrates the project’s ongoing commitment to responsive development that addresses community needs while maintaining the stability required for production research data repositories.

Version 6.4 introduces several valuable features that improve the user experience and expand administrative capabilities. Enhancements to data upload and download workflows make it easier for researchers to deposit and retrieve their datasets, while improvements to metadata handling support more comprehensive data documentation. New administrative tools provide repository managers with better oversight and control, and enhanced monitoring capabilities help identify and resolve issues proactively. The release also includes updates to external tool integrations, enabling researchers to connect their data with analysis platforms and visualization tools.

item.title: Dataverse Software 6.6 Release
item.description:
The Dataverse Project announces the release of version 6.6, delivering important bug fixes and new features that continue to advance the platform’s capabilities as the world’s most widely adopted open-source research data repository software. This release reflects the collaborative efforts of the global Dataverse community, combining contributions from developers at institutions worldwide to improve functionality, stability, and user experience.

Version 6.6 introduces features that enhance both researcher and administrator workflows. Improvements to data management capabilities make it easier to organize, describe, and share research datasets according to community standards and funder requirements. Enhanced search and discovery features help users find relevant data more efficiently, while new APIs and integration points support more sophisticated connections with institutional systems and research tools. Administrative enhancements provide repository managers with improved monitoring, reporting, and configuration options to tailor their Dataverse installations to local needs.

item.title: Digitized Canon: Intisar Rabb on SHARIAsource
item.description:
Professor Intisar Rabb’s career as a legal scholar began during a joint program at Yale Law School and Princeton, focusing on comparative issues in Islamic law. At Harvard, she has developed SHARIAsource—a digital platform that makes primary sources of Islamic legal tradition accessible to researchers worldwide.

SHARIAsource represents an ambitious effort to digitize and organize centuries of Islamic legal texts, judicial opinions, and scholarly commentary, creating a searchable database that can support comparative legal research. The project leverages IQSS’s data infrastructure and computational resources.

item.title: Douglas Richardson Awarded Lifetime Achievement Honors
item.description:
Douglas Richardson, Distinguished Researcher at Harvard’s Center for Geographic Analysis (CGA), received Lifetime Achievement Honors from the American Association of Geographers. The award recognizes his decades of contributions to geographic information science and its applications.

Richardson has been instrumental in advancing the use of GIS technology in academic research, working to make geospatial tools accessible to scholars across disciplines. His work at CGA has helped establish Harvard as a leader in geographic analysis and spatial data infrastructure.

item.title: DSM recognizes moral injury, thanks to Human Flourishing Program research
item.description:
During the COVID-19 pandemic, frontline physicians, nurses, and first responders faced impossible choices—who would receive a ventilator, whether to keep working without adequate protection, and how to protect their families while serving the public. In those moments, moral injury, a phenomenon once linked primarily to the military, became an everyday reality in healthcare.

Recognizing this urgent need, the Human Flourishing Program at IQSS, directed by Dr. Tyler VanderWeele, launched a global initiative to advance research and equip leaders across healthcare, military, and first responder sectors with tools for identifying moral injury and promoting healing. Moral injury occurs when individuals in high-stakes roles must navigate wrenching ethical dilemmas where no option feels right, leaving lasting scars on conscience and well-being. The consequences include persistent anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress, substance use, and an erosion of trust and meaning.

item.title: Explore the CGAs 2026 Training Schedule
item.description:
The Center for Geographic Analysis has unveiled a completely revamped training workshop schedule for 2026, offering Harvard community members an extensive array of geospatial technology and data science courses. All workshops are free for Harvard ID holders and cover essential tools and platforms including ArcGIS, QGIS, Python, R, KNIME, and many other technologies used in geographic analysis and spatial data science.

The 2026 training program reflects CGA’s commitment to keeping pace with rapidly evolving geospatial technologies and methodologies. The schedule includes both introductory courses for those new to GIS and advanced workshops for experienced users looking to expand their skills. Topics range from fundamental mapping and spatial analysis techniques to sophisticated applications of machine learning, remote sensing, and big data analytics in geographic contexts. The workshops are designed to serve researchers across all disciplines who work with spatial data in their research.

item.title: Fall Workshops from Data Science Services
item.description:
IQSS Data Science Services is excited to announce a comprehensive schedule of 12 free data science workshops for the fall semester, offering Harvard researchers valuable opportunities to develop new skills and enhance their computational research capabilities. These workshops cover a diverse range of topics essential to modern quantitative research, from programming fundamentals to advanced statistical methods and specialized tools for data analysis.

The fall workshop series is designed to serve researchers at various skill levels, from those just beginning to incorporate computational methods into their work to experienced practitioners looking to expand their toolkit. Topics span popular programming languages like R and Python, version control with Git, data visualization techniques, machine learning methods, text analysis, and more. Each workshop provides hands-on, practical instruction that participants can immediately apply to their own research projects. The workshops are taught by experienced data science consultants who understand the specific needs and challenges facing academic researchers.

item.title: Following in Ancient Footsteps: Jeff Blossom Maps an Explorer Walk
item.description:
Jeff Blossom of the Center for Geographic Analysis collaborated for six years on a National Geographic Society project—Paul Salopek’s Out of Eden Walk, an intercontinental journey retracing the path of human migration out of Africa. Blossom provided GIS expertise to map and document the walk.

The project required creating detailed cartographic representations of diverse terrains across multiple continents, integrating satellite imagery, ground-truth data, and historical geographic information. Blossom’s work transformed Salopek’s physical journey into a rich digital archive accessible to researchers and the public.

item.title: Forgotten Graves: A New Lens on Colonial Slavery
item.description:
by Courtney Hayes

Jason Ur, Stephen Phillips Professor of Archaeology and Ethnology and IQSS affiliate, is working to unearth histories long hidden in Cambridge. Known for pioneering archaeological work in the Middle East, Ur has turned his attention to Cambridge’s Old Burying Ground, where questions about segregation and slavery have remained buried for centuries. Using drones, ground-penetrating radar, and a geospatial database, he is re-mapping the past and challenging how we understand it.

item.title: From Harvard Research to National Security Innovation: The Story of Thresher
item.description:
In 2015, Weatherhead University Professor Gary King co-founded Thresher, a company born from his academic research at Harvard’s Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS).

Thresher’s origins trace back to two groundbreaking research programs. The first focused on censorship and fabrication of Chinese social media posts, conducted with then graduate students Patrick Lam (now at Airbnb), Jennifer Pan (now professor at Stanford), and Molly Roberts (now professor at UCSD). While analyzing data from an earlier startup, Crimson Hexagon (now Brandwatch), King and his collaborators discovered a method to download Chinese social media content before it could be censored by the government. This serendipitous finding gave them access to the entire corpus of posts Chinese citizens were not allowed to read, becoming the foundation for deeper analysis of online information manipulation.

item.title: From NASA to the Enterprise: LISH Expands Programs
item.description:
The Laboratory for Innovation Science at Harvard (LISH), one of IQSS’s scientific programs, has experienced unprecedented growth over the past two years. Originally evolving from the NASA Tournament Lab, LISH now works with partner organizations across government, industry, and non-profit sectors on crowd-sourced problem solving.

Under its new expanded mandate, LISH conducts research on how organizations can leverage distributed intelligence, open innovation, and AI to solve complex problems. The lab’s work combines rigorous experimental methods with practical applications in areas ranging from space exploration to drug discovery.

item.title: From the Directors: A Fall Update
item.description:
This last year has brought a lot of exciting and transformative advancements at IQSS and across the social sciences. Here are a few highlights.

Dataverse Project

The Dataverse Project continued its community growth worldwide, reaching 100 installations, thousands of dataverses, and millions of dataset downloads. IQSS is undertaking a major restructuring of the Dataverse UI as a “single-page application,” which will enable custom UIs, integration with other applications, and make novel statistical innovations instantly available to users. The Dataverse Project is also part of an effort by the Office of Data Science Strategy (ODSS) at the NIH to launch a new data curation, sharing, and interoperability initiative across repositories. The team is also testing experimental code leveraging large language model (LLM) applications within Dataverse, allowing users to interact with and ask questions about Dataverse conversationally.

item.title: From the Directors: An Update on Dataverse
item.description:
IQSS leadership shared an update on the Dataverse Project, reflecting on deep dives into the many areas and layers of their work. The institute has been looking for ways to maximize its impact and position itself for even greater success in research data management.

The directors discussed strategic priorities for the Dataverse platform, including expanding its global reach, improving user experience, and strengthening the community of contributors who maintain and extend the open-source software. These efforts come as research data sharing requirements from funders and journals continue to grow.

item.title: Future of Media Project: Who Owns Canadian News Media?
item.description:
IQSS Future of Media Project fellow Heidi Legg published a Canadian Media Ownership Index detailing ownership and digital consumption patterns of Canada’s top 125 news outlets. The research comes at a critical time for media industry analysis, as ownership concentration raises questions about editorial independence and information diversity.

The index provides transparency about who controls the information channels that Canadians rely on for news, revealing patterns of consolidation and the growing influence of digital platforms on traditional media business models. This data-driven approach to media analysis reflects the Future of Media Project’s mission at IQSS.

item.title: Gary King instrumental in new academic-industry collaboration model
item.description:
IQSS Director Gary King and Nate Persily of Stanford Law School developed a new model for academic collaboration with industry, addressing the growing tension between researchers’ need for data and companies’ obligations to protect user privacy. Their working paper proposes frameworks for structured data access.

The model acknowledges that most data about people is now held by private companies rather than governments or academic institutions, creating a fundamental challenge for social science research. King and Persily argue that incentive-compatible arrangements can benefit both parties—companies gain research insights while academics access data that would otherwise be unavailable.

item.title: Gary King Receives 2019 Excellence in Mentoring Award
item.description:
The Society for Political Methodology named Gary King, Albert J. Weatherhead III University Professor and IQSS Director, as the 2019 recipient of the Excellence in Mentoring Award. The award recognizes sustained contributions to training the next generation of political methodologists.

King has mentored dozens of PhD students who have gone on to faculty positions at leading universities, building a network of scholars who advance quantitative methods in political science. His mentoring approach emphasizes both technical rigor and the practical skills needed to build successful academic careers.

item.title: Gary King: A Welcome Back Update
item.description:
Dear: IQSS Staff and Affiliates
From: Gary King, Director IQSS
Subj: A welcome back update
Date: 8/19/2021

King opens by noting the IQSS community has been away from CGIS for nearly two years, with exceptions for the Client Support Services team and others. He announces the official return the week of August 23rd.

He describes the pre-pandemic intellectual community at IQSS as unique “not only among Harvard groups, but throughout academia” and outlines goals to preserve the virtual community, reconstitute the physical one, and build on lessons learned about workplace flexibility.

item.title: Global Flourishing Study Releases Wave One Open Research Data
item.description:
Wave one of the Global Flourishing Study dataset is now available to researchers, representing a partnership among Baylor University, Harvard’s Human Flourishing Program, Gallup, and the Center for Open Science. The dataset covers approximately 200,000 participants across 22 countries.

The study measures six domains of flourishing: happiness and life satisfaction, mental and physical health, meaning and purpose, character and virtue, close social relationships, and financial and material stability. This comprehensive approach moves beyond pathology-focused measures to capture what it means to truly thrive.

item.title: Global Flourishing Study Wave 2 Extended Datasets Available Now
item.description:
The Human Flourishing Program at Harvard University has released Wave 2 data from the Global Flourishing Study, marking a significant milestone in one of the most comprehensive longitudinal research efforts on human well-being ever undertaken. This new wave of data is now freely available to researchers worldwide through the Center for Open Science, continuing the study’s commitment to open science and collaborative research.

The Wave 2 extended datasets build upon the groundbreaking first wave by tracking the same cohort of approximately 200,000 participants across 22 countries over time. This longitudinal design allows researchers to examine how flourishing changes across different life stages and in response to various life events and circumstances. The data captures information across six key domains of human flourishing: happiness and life satisfaction, physical and mental health, meaning and purpose, character and virtue, close social relationships, and financial and material stability.

item.title: Harvard Climate Justice Design Fellowship Announces Inaugural Fellows
item.description:
The Harvard Climate Justice Design Fellowship selected seven inaugural fellows who are leaders in environmental justice advocacy, organizing, and law from across the United States. The program, launched by IQSS, supports climate justice work through data science and design.

Fellows participate in intensive training on data analysis, visualization, and design thinking, then apply these skills to climate justice challenges in their home communities. The program bridges the gap between technical capability and grassroots organizing, ensuring that data tools serve community needs.

item.title: Harvard IQSS and Microsoft announce major OpenDP collaboration
item.description:
Gary King led IQSS’s announcement of a major collaboration with Microsoft to build a platform ensuring data is kept private while remaining useful for research. The partnership aims to develop open-source differential privacy tools accessible to researchers and organizations handling sensitive information.

The collaboration brings together Harvard’s theoretical expertise in privacy-preserving computation with Microsoft’s engineering resources and practical experience deploying privacy technologies at scale. Together, they aim to create tools that make differential privacy practical for everyday use by researchers and data analysts.

item.title: Harvard Thinking - Preserving learning in the age of AI shortcuts
item.description:
In an episode of Harvard Thinking, IQSS affiliate Michael Brenner explores one of the most pressing questions facing educators today: how has artificial intelligence affected learning and critical thinking, and how can these powerful tools be embraced to enhance rather than diminish educational outcomes? Brenner, the Glover Professor of Applied Mathematics and Applied Physics at Harvard, brings both technical expertise and pedagogical insight to this critical discussion.

Brenner examines the double-edged nature of AI in educational contexts. While AI tools can provide instant answers and automate routine tasks, there is growing concern that students may become overly reliant on these technologies, bypassing the cognitive struggle that leads to deep understanding and skill development. The conversation addresses how educators can design learning experiences that leverage AI’s capabilities while ensuring students still develop fundamental thinking skills, creativity, and problem-solving abilities that remain uniquely human.

item.title: Harvesting Data From the Field to the Cloud: The 2024 Dataverse Community Meeting
item.description:
The 10th anniversary Dataverse Community Meeting was held March 4-8 at CIMMYT (International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center) in Texcoco, Mexico. The location reflected Dataverse’s growing importance in agricultural and development research.

The meeting focused on collaboration and the future of data systems, bringing together Dataverse users and developers from institutions around the world. Sessions covered topics from repository administration to data curation best practices, with particular attention to the needs of researchers in the Global South.

item.title: How forgiving can improve well-being
item.description:
Researchers at the Human Flourishing Program have published groundbreaking findings revealing a strong connection between the regular practice of forgiveness and significant improvements in psychological well-being and character development. The study adds to a growing body of evidence suggesting that forgiveness is not merely a moral or spiritual concept but a powerful contributor to mental health and personal growth that can be cultivated through intentional practice.

The research examined individuals who engaged in regular forgiveness practices over time, tracking changes in various dimensions of psychological well-being including life satisfaction, positive emotions, depression, anxiety, and overall mental health. Beyond these psychological benefits, the study also documented meaningful changes in character traits, suggesting that the practice of forgiveness can fundamentally reshape how individuals relate to themselves and others. These findings indicate that forgiveness functions as more than just a way to resolve specific grievances, instead serving as a transformative practice that enhances overall quality of life.

item.title: How to regulate AI
item.description: IQSS affiliate David Y. Yang, Professor of Economics at Harvard University, has joined other leading experts in addressing one of the most pressing policy questions of our time: how to effectively regulate artificial intelligence development and deployment. As US legislators pass measures at both state and federal levels, the debate over AI regulation has intensified, with scholars and policymakers grappling with balancing innovation against risks ranging from privacy violations and algorithmic bias to potential existential threats from advanced AI systems.

item.title: Human Flourishing Program Launches Global Flourishing Study
item.description: The Human Flourishing Program at Harvard’s IQSS has launched the ambitious Global Flourishing Study, a groundbreaking international research initiative designed to explore fundamental questions about what it means to live well across diverse cultures and societies. This comprehensive, multi-year study represents one of the most extensive efforts ever undertaken to understand human well-being in all its dimensions, moving beyond narrow measures of happiness or economic prosperity to examine flourishing holistically.

item.title: Human Flourishing Program, Baylor, & Gallup Release Wave 1 Findings
item.description: The Global Flourishing Study (GFS), a multi-year collaborative research effort conducted by the Human Flourishing Program at Harvard University, Baylor’s Institute for Studies of Religion, Gallup, and the Center for Open Science, has released its First Wave findings on human flourishing. This represents one of the most comprehensive studies of human well-being ever undertaken, defining flourishing as “living in a state in which all aspects of a person’s life are good.”

item.title: In touch with our emotions, finally
item.description:
Jennifer Lerner, IQSS affiliate and Bradshaw Professor of Public Policy at Harvard Kennedy School, examines how emotion and gender factor into decision making. Her research challenges long-held assumptions about rationality and shows that emotions can both help and hinder good judgment depending on the context.

Lerner’s work has revealed systematic patterns in how specific emotions influence economic decisions, risk assessment, and policy preferences. For example, anger tends to make people more optimistic about risks and more punitive in their judgments, while sadness promotes more careful deliberation but can also lead to impatience in financial decisions.

item.title: Introducing Extraordinary Claims, Extraordinary Evidence
item.description:
by Elizabeth Salazar

The Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS) launched a program offering Harvard faculty exceptional help in providing extraordinary evidence for their extraordinary claims. Called Extraordinary Claims, Extraordinary Evidence (ECEE), the program aims to catalyze rigorous research that would shift dominant scientific positions significantly.

The program recognizes that many extraordinary claims are “explicitly contrarian positions” that, if validated, reveal flawed assumptions or unsupported conclusions in existing research. Others address subjects not actively studied—topics scholars incorrectly assume are understood or consider unworthy of investigation—yet which hold potential for major shifts in a field’s dominant view.

item.title: Introducing Version 0.4 of the OpenDP Library
item.description:
The OpenDP Project is pleased to announce the release of version 0.4 of the OpenDP Library, introducing important new measurements and significant floating point improvements that enhance both the functionality and reliability of the differential privacy toolkit. This release represents substantial progress toward making differential privacy practical and trustworthy for protecting sensitive data in real-world applications.

Version 0.4 expands the library’s capabilities with additional measurements that enable new types of differentially private analyses. These new statistical tools provide data practitioners with more options for extracting insights from sensitive datasets while maintaining rigorous privacy guarantees. The floating point improvements address critical technical challenges in implementing differential privacy algorithms, ensuring that computations maintain accuracy and reliability even when dealing with numerical precision issues that can arise in complex calculations.

item.title: IQSS Affiliate-Led HDSI Receives Sloan Grant
item.description:
The Harvard Data Science Initiative (HDSI), led by IQSS affiliate Professor Xiao-Li Meng, has received a prestigious $1 million grant from the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation to advance research at the intersection of causal inference and machine learning. This significant funding recognizes the importance of integrating these two powerful approaches to data analysis and supports HDSI’s mission to advance data science methods that address pressing societal challenges.

The grant will support research that bridges causal inference—the rigorous statistical framework for understanding cause-and-effect relationships—with machine learning techniques that excel at finding patterns in complex, high-dimensional data. This integration is crucial for many applications where researchers need not just to predict outcomes but to understand what interventions will actually cause desired changes. The work has important implications across fields including public health, education policy, economics, and social science research.

item.title: IQSS and Collaborators Announce How We Feel Project
item.description:
IQSS, Pinterest, and Harvard TH Chan School of Public Health scientists collaborated to address the gap in recorded cases of the novel coronavirus in the United States through the How We Feel Project. The initiative launched in early April 2020 as COVID-19 testing remained severely limited.

The project developed a mobile application that collected self-reported health data from users, creating a real-time picture of symptom prevalence across the country that complemented official testing data. This crowd-sourced epidemiological approach leveraged smartphone technology to gather information at a scale and speed that traditional surveillance systems could not match.

item.title: IQSS Celebrates 20 years
item.description:
The year 2025 marks the 20th anniversary of the Institute for Quantitative Social Science at Harvard University. The milestone was celebrated at the Center for Geographic and International Studies (CGIS) with faculty, staff, and students from across the IQSS community who work to advance research at Harvard and other institutions.

Over two decades, IQSS has developed into a hub where leading minds in data and social science collaborate on solutions to pressing societal issues. As the institute marks this milestone and reflects on its accomplishments, projects, and innovations, it is also looking ahead—including new programs and partnerships that will launch at IQSS.

item.title: IQSS Co-Hosts Comparative Politics and Formal Theory Conference
item.description: IQSS co-hosted the 8th Comparative Politics and Formal Theory (CPFT) Conference at Harvard, bringing together political scientists who use formal modeling and rigorous empirical methods to study comparative politics. This specialized conference provides a venue for researchers working at the intersection of game theory, formal models, and comparative political analysis to present work, receive feedback, and build connections across institutions. The gathering reflects IQSS’s commitment to supporting methodologically sophisticated political science research.

item.title: IQSS Data Science Hosts First Dataverse Community Meeting
item.description:
IQSS Data Science Services hosted the inaugural Dataverse Community Meeting, bringing together repository administrators, developers, and data professionals from institutions worldwide for the first gathering centered on the Dataverse platform. This historic event marked an important milestone in the evolution of Dataverse from a Harvard project into a global open-source community, creating opportunities for knowledge sharing, collaborative problem-solving, and collective planning for the platform’s future development.

The first Dataverse Community Meeting featured presentations about repository implementations, technical workshops, discussions of data curation challenges, and planning sessions for future software development. Attendees shared experiences managing Dataverse repositories in diverse institutional contexts, from small university libraries to large international collaborations. The gathering enabled face-to-face connections among people who had primarily interacted through online channels, strengthening relationships and building the sense of community essential for successful open-source projects.

item.title: IQSS Dataverse joins NIH-funded collaboration on climate change and health
item.description: IQSS’s Dataverse Project has joined an NIH-funded collaboration focused on climate change and health, bringing research data repository expertise to a major interdisciplinary initiative examining the health impacts of environmental change. This partnership reflects growing recognition that climate change poses profound threats to human health through pathways including extreme heat, air quality degradation, infectious disease patterns, food security, and mental health impacts. Understanding and addressing these challenges requires integration of climate science, epidemiology, and health research—and the data infrastructure to support such work.

item.title: IQSS Director on Science Magazine Podcast
item.description: IQSS Director Gary King appeared on the Science Magazine podcast to discuss his research on media influence on public discourse and how new data sources and computational methods are transforming understanding of how information spreads and shapes public opinion. The conversation explored King’s pioneering work analyzing large-scale social media data to understand patterns of censorship, propaganda, and information manipulation, particularly in authoritarian contexts where traditional research methods face significant barriers.

item.title: IQSS Establishes Digital Lab for Social Science
item.description: IQSS has established the Digital Lab for the Social Sciences (DLABSS), a dedicated space and program designed to support computationally intensive social science research and provide researchers with access to expertise, tools, and collaborative environment for digital scholarship. The lab represents IQSS’s commitment to advancing methodological innovation by creating infrastructure that enables researchers to work with new data sources, apply cutting-edge analytical methods, and collaborate on projects that push the boundaries of traditional social science.

item.title: IQSS Faculty and Student Question Replicability Crisis
item.description:
IQSS faculty members and graduate students have entered a major scientific debate by challenging influential claims about the so-called replicability crisis in psychology. Their work questions the interpretation of a large-scale replication study that appeared to show widespread failure to reproduce findings from published psychology research, arguing that the original analysis may have overstated problems with replicability and potentially mischaracterized the state of psychological science.

The IQSS researchers employ sophisticated statistical methods to reanalyze replication study data, demonstrating that different analytical approaches and assumptions can lead to substantially different conclusions about replication success rates. Their work highlights methodological challenges in assessing replication, including how to define replication success, account for statistical power in both original and replication studies, and interpret differences between original and replication effect sizes. This intervention in a high-profile scientific debate exemplifies IQSS’s role in advancing methodological rigor and statistical understanding across the sciences.

item.title: IQSS Honors Staff Service Milestones
item.description:
The Institute for Quantitative Social Science recently gathered to celebrate staff members who have reached important service milestones. At IQSS they value their community, and their dedicated staff is at the heart of that community. This annual recognition honors colleagues who in 2025 reached a work anniversary of 10 years or more, in 5-year increments. Their long-term commitment, talent, and collaboration make our work possible.

This year’s IQSS Service Award recipients are:

item.title: IQSS launches Climate Justice Design Fellowship program
item.description:
IQSS has launched the Climate Justice Design Fellowship program, an innovative initiative that brings together data science, design thinking, and climate justice principles to address environmental challenges in ways that center equity and community needs. This fellowship program reflects growing recognition that climate change disproportionately affects marginalized communities and that addressing climate challenges requires not just technical solutions but approaches grounded in justice and participatory design.

The Climate Justice Design Fellowship supports interdisciplinary teams combining expertise in data science, environmental science, design, and social science to develop tools, visualizations, and interventions that help communities understand and respond to climate impacts. Fellows work on projects that might include mapping climate vulnerability in environmental justice communities, creating accessible data visualization tools for community organizations, or developing platforms that amplify community voices in climate policy discussions. The program emphasizes participatory approaches that involve affected communities as partners rather than subjects of research.

item.title: IQSS launches Sid cloud research software
item.description:
IQSS has launched Sid, a groundbreaking cloud-agnostic multi-user research computing platform that revolutionizes how researchers access and use high-performance computing resources. Named in honor of Sidney Verba, the pioneering political scientist who championed making research resources accessible to all scholars, Sid provides an intuitive web-based interface that democratizes access to powerful computing capabilities without requiring deep technical expertise in command-line computing or system administration.

Sid represents a fundamental shift in research computing philosophy, moving from the traditional model where researchers must learn complex technical skills to access computing power, to one where sophisticated capabilities are presented through user-friendly interfaces. The platform supports diverse workflows including interactive data analysis, batch computing jobs, visualization, and collaborative work, all accessible through a web browser from anywhere. Its cloud-agnostic architecture means researchers can leverage computing resources from different providers, avoiding vendor lock-in while maintaining consistent workflows.

item.title: IQSS Welcomes Dr. Stefano Iacus
item.description:
The Institute for Quantitative Social Science is delighted to welcome Dr. Stefano Iacus as Senior Research Scientist, bringing extensive expertise in computational statistics, time series analysis, and statistical software development to IQSS’s research and methodological innovation initiatives. Dr. Iacus’s arrival strengthens IQSS’s capacity to support cutting-edge quantitative research and advance the development of statistical tools used by scholars worldwide.

Dr. Iacus joins IQSS with an impressive background spanning academic research, open-source software development, and practical applications of statistical methods to real-world problems. His expertise in simulation-based inference, computational methods, and statistical software has contributed to major research computing projects and the development of widely used R packages. His work bridges theoretical statistics and applied data science, making sophisticated methods accessible to researchers across disciplines.

item.title: IQSS Welcomes New Executive Director Elizabeth Jackson
item.description:
IQSS announced the arrival of its new Executive Director, Elizabeth Jackson, who comes from Greater Portland Health in Maine and from Harvard’s T.H. Chan School of Public Health’s Department of Global Health and Population.

Jackson has built a career aligned with IQSS’s mission. She most recently served as Chief Administrative Officer of The Greater Portland Health Center and has engaged in health system and community strengthening work with partners across Africa, Asia, and Latin America. She has also led trauma-informed resilience trainings.

item.title: IQSS Welcomes WashU into Magaro Peer Pre-Review Consortium
item.description:
by Liz Salazar
