The Institute for Quantitative Social Science at Harvard University is expanding the Alexander and Diviya Magaro Peer Pre-Review Program—a service designed to improve scholarship by speeding up the journal submission process—into a multi-institutional consortium. The Weidenbaum Center on the Economy, Government, and Public Policy at Washington University in St. Louis is the first affiliate to partner with IQSS, adding its own network of associate editors and peer reviewers to Harvard’s existing cohort. Washington University professors Gaetano Antinolfi and Ian Filmore will work with senior preceptor Chase Harrison at IQSS to coordinate the review process for submitted drafts.

item.title: Is AI dulling our minds?
item.description: As artificial intelligence tools become increasingly integrated into educational settings and professional workflows, questions about their impact on critical thinking and cognitive development have moved to the forefront of academic discourse. IQSS faculty affiliate Dan Levy, Senior Lecturer in Public Policy at the Harvard Kennedy School, joined other Harvard faculty members in a thoughtful discussion examining whether AI will ultimately help or harm the human capacity for critical thinking.

item.title: Is the Two-Parent Family the Great Equalizer?
item.description: Research from IQSS scholars examines a provocative question in debates about inequality and family structure: does marriage and the two-parent family serve as a great equalizer, helping to reduce economic and social disparities? This question sits at the intersection of research on family structure, economic mobility, and social stratification, with important implications for how we understand pathways out of poverty and the role of family policy in addressing inequality.

item.title: James Robins Recognized with COPSS Distinguished Achievement Award
item.description:
James Robins—IQSS faculty affiliate and Mitchell L. and Robin LaFoley Dong Professor of Epidemiology at the TH Chan School of Public Health—has been recognized by the Committee of Presidents of Statistical Societies (COPSS) with the 2025 Distinguished Achievement Award and Lectureship.

The prestigious annual award, previously known as the RA Fisher Award and Lectureship from 1963–2019, recognizes “outstanding contributions to statistical methods that have had significant impact on scientific investigation.”

item.title: Jeff Frieden Traded to New York! Celebrating 30 Years at Harvard
item.description:
A celebration of Jeffry Frieden—a die-hard Yankees fan—and his 30 years at Harvard. Frieden, an IQSS faculty affiliate and Stanfield Professor of International Peace, has been a leading scholar of international political economy throughout his distinguished career.

Frieden’s research examines how economic interests shape political outcomes, with particular focus on exchange rate politics, financial crises, and the political economy of European integration. His work combines rigorous quantitative analysis with deep historical knowledge, exemplifying the kind of interdisciplinary scholarship IQSS supports.

item.title: Ji Su Yoo Awarded P.D. Soros Fellowship
item.description:
Ji Su Yoo, a Harvard alumna and IQSS Fellow, was named a 2020 recipient of the Paul & Daisy Soros Fellowship for New Americans. The prestigious fellowship supports outstanding graduate students who are immigrants or children of immigrants in the United States.

Yoo’s research focuses on technology in decision-making, exploring how algorithmic systems affect fairness, accountability, and transparency in high-stakes contexts. Her work at the intersection of computer science and public policy reflects IQSS’s interdisciplinary approach to studying technology’s social impact.

item.title: Join Dataverse for the GREI Collaborative Webinar Series
item.description:
The Dataverse Project invites the research data management community to participate in the GREI Collaborative Webinar Series, which focuses on repository resources and best practices specifically tailored for NIH-funded research. This series provides valuable guidance for researchers, repository administrators, and data managers working to meet the National Institutes of Health’s data sharing policies and requirements while leveraging Dataverse as a trusted repository platform.

The GREI (Generalist Repository Ecosystem Initiative) Collaborative brings together major research data repositories to provide coordinated support for biomedical researchers navigating data sharing requirements. The webinar series covers practical topics including metadata best practices, preparing datasets for sharing, understanding access controls and restricted data considerations, and ensuring long-term preservation of research data. These sessions are particularly valuable as NIH continues to strengthen its data sharing policies, requiring researchers to plan for and execute robust data management from the beginning of their projects.

item.title: Kenneth Shepsle Awarded William H. Riker Prize
item.description:
Kenneth A. Shepsle, IQSS affiliate and George D. Markham Professor of Government, received the 2023 William H. Riker prize in Political Science from the University of Rochester. The prize recognizes lifetime contributions to the scientific study of politics through formal theory and its applications.

Shepsle’s career has been defined by groundbreaking work on legislative institutions, congressional committees, and the structure of political choice. His research on institutional design and legislative bargaining has fundamentally shaped how political scientists understand how rules and procedures affect policy outcomes.

item.title: Kosuke Imai Returns to Harvard
item.description:
by Jonathan Colburn

Changing Seasons: Kosuke Imai Returns to Harvard to Expand the Reach of Quantitative Social Science at Harvard and Abroad

When he last occupied office space at Harvard, Kosuke Imai, IQSS’s newest resident faculty affiliate, had a decidedly different experience than he does in his current location at CGIS Knafel overlooking Cambridge Street.

“IQSS didn’t exist yet, but its predecessor was housed in a little yellow house where the Ukrainian Research Institute is,” Imai recalls. His graduate student office was upstairs in the attic, and demolition of the old building nearby meant that construction equipment would shake his entire workspace.

item.title: Latanya Sweeney Appointed to FTC
item.description:
IQSS affiliate Latanya Sweeney, director of the Data Privacy Lab and Professor of Government, was appointed to join the Federal Trade Commission as Chief Technologist in January 2014. The appointment brought deep technical expertise on data privacy to the nation’s primary consumer protection agency.

Sweeney’s research has demonstrated how supposedly anonymous data can be re-identified, revealing fundamental vulnerabilities in how organizations handle personal information. Her work has influenced privacy regulations and corporate data practices worldwide, making her uniquely qualified for the FTC role.

item.title: Latanya Sweeney Featured in Forbes List
item.description:
IQSS faculty affiliate and Data Privacy Lab director Latanya Sweeney was profiled in a Forbes list of Incredible Women Advancing A.I. Research. The recognition highlighted her pioneering work at the intersection of artificial intelligence, data privacy, and public policy.

Sweeney’s contributions to the field include demonstrating that 87% of the U.S. population could be uniquely identified by just their ZIP code, birth date, and gender—a finding that fundamentally changed how organizations think about data anonymization. Her research continues to expose hidden privacy risks in commercial and government data systems.

item.title: Latanya Sweeney Technology Science Research Collaboration
item.description:
In 2015, Latanya Sweeney received a $250,000 MacArthur Foundation grant to engage scholars and students investigating unforeseen consequences of technology. The funding supported the Technology Science Research Collaboration Network, bringing together researchers across disciplines to study how technology affects society.

The network connects scholars who might otherwise work in isolation, facilitating collaborative research on topics including algorithmic bias, surveillance, data privacy, and digital discrimination. This interdisciplinary approach recognizes that technology’s social impacts cannot be understood through any single disciplinary lens.

item.title: Launching the Differential Privacy Deployments Registry
item.description:
OpenDP at IQSS has launched the Differential Privacy Deployments Registry, a groundbreaking initiative designed to empower organizations and researchers to share detailed information about their differential privacy implementations. This new resource addresses a critical need in the data privacy community by creating a centralized platform where practitioners can document their approaches, methodologies, and lessons learned from real-world deployments of differential privacy technologies.

Differential privacy has emerged as the gold standard for protecting individual privacy while enabling valuable data analysis, but understanding how to implement it effectively in practice remains challenging. The registry provides a venue for organizations to transparently share their deployment strategies, parameter choices, and practical considerations, helping others learn from existing implementations. By documenting both successes and challenges, the registry aims to accelerate the adoption of differential privacy across various sectors including government, healthcare, technology, and research.

item.title: Making Historical Figure Search 500X Faster
item.description: The China Biographical Database (CBDB) team redesigned their name indexing method to make text analysis research considerably faster.

item.title: Mapping Chinese History: The China Biographical Database
item.description:
The China Biographical Database (CBDB) aims at identifying and cataloging individuals across millennia of Chinese society and their shared connections. This ambitious digital humanities project, based at IQSS, represents one of the largest biographical databases ever constructed for historical research.

CBDB contains biographical information from Chinese historical records spanning the 7th through 19th centuries, enabling researchers to conduct statistical analysis, social network analysis, and spatial analysis of historical Chinese society. The database reveals patterns of social mobility, political networks, and cultural transmission that traditional historical methods could not detect.

item.title: Melissa Dell Wins 2020 Clark Medal
item.description:
IQSS faculty affiliate Melissa Dell received the American Economic Association’s 2020 John Bates Clark Medal, granted annually to an economist under 40 judged to have made the most significant contributions to economic thought and knowledge. The medal is often considered a precursor to the Nobel Prize in Economics.

Dell’s research examines how historical institutions—from colonial forced labor systems to authoritarian regimes—shape economic development over centuries. Her innovative use of geographic discontinuities, historical records, and computational methods has produced some of the most compelling evidence for the long-run effects of institutions on economic outcomes.

item.title: Memorious: Building Infinite Memory with AI
item.description:
Forgetting is universal—conversations, names, moments all slip away. But what if it were optional? That’s the ambitious vision behind Memorious, a company founded by Spandan Madan and Gabriel Kreiman at Harvard. Their goal is to reinvent human memory using AI, building tools that allow people to recall experiences, conversations, and information instantly without searching or prompting.

From Research to Reality

The science powering Memorious originates from Gabriel’s lab at Harvard. As a Professor of Neuroscience and AI at Harvard Medical School, Gabriel has spent decades researching how the brain encodes and retrieves memory. His lab’s findings formed the technological foundation for the company. He partnered with Spandan Madan, a Harvard Computer Science PhD with extensive machine learning experience from Microsoft, Adobe, and IIT. Together they recognized the chance to merge neuroscience and AI to create what they term “Ambient Intelligence.”

item.title: Merce Crosas Departs to be Secretary of Open Government in Catalonia
item.description:
After 15 years at Harvard as Chief Data Science and Technology Officer at IQSS, Merce Crosas departed to serve as Secretary of Open Government in Catalonia, Spain. Her departure marked the end of an era in which she helped build IQSS’s data science infrastructure from the ground up.

Crosas led the development of the Dataverse Project, growing it from a Harvard-centric tool to a globally adopted research data platform with over 100 installations worldwide. Under her leadership, the project became a model for how academic institutions can create sustainable open-source infrastructure that serves the broader research community.

item.title: Mind2Market Demo Day
item.description:
Harvard researchers gathered for the Mind2Market Demo Day, a showcase event highlighting cutting-edge artificial intelligence projects that have reached the stage where they are ready to transition from academic research to market-ready ventures. The event featured several teams from the IQSS Startup Foundry, Harvard’s initiative designed to help social science researchers transform their innovations into commercial applications with real-world impact.

The Demo Day provided researchers with a platform to pitch their AI-powered projects to potential partners, investors, and collaborators interested in bringing academic innovations to market. Teams presented projects spanning various domains of artificial intelligence and social science, demonstrating how research developed within Harvard’s academic environment can address practical challenges and create value in the commercial sector. The presentations showcased the maturity of these ventures and their readiness for the next stage of development.

item.title: NASA Tournament Lab Launches Asteroid Hunter Contest
item.description:
The NASA Tournament Lab at IQSS challenged competitors to develop better asteroid detection methods, leveraging crowd-sourced innovation to address a critical space safety challenge. The lab, a collaboration among NASA, Harvard Business School, and TopCoder, uses competition-based approaches to solve complex technical problems.

The Asteroid Hunter Contest asked participants to develop algorithms that could identify potentially hazardous asteroids from astronomical observation data more accurately and efficiently than existing methods. By opening the problem to a global community of problem-solvers, the contest aimed to tap expertise beyond traditional astronomy and aerospace engineering.

item.title: Nate Silver to Lead Political Analytics 2017
item.description:
The Center for American Political Studies announced the second annual Political Analytics Conference on March 31, 2017, with FiveThirtyEight founder Nate Silver as the keynote speaker. The conference, co-sponsored by IQSS, brings together academics, journalists, and practitioners working at the intersection of data science and politics.

The conference examines how quantitative methods are transforming political campaigns, journalism, and governance. Topics range from election forecasting and survey methodology to social media analytics and political communication strategies.

item.title: Neru Health - From Sleep Challenges to Scaling Chronic Care with AI
item.description:
When Neru Health first came to the IQSS Startup Foundry, they had a big idea: transform the way people solve sleep problems. Millions struggle with conditions like sleep apnea, and while treatments exist, staying consistent with therapy is notoriously difficult. Neru’s early vision centered on improving patient engagement and adherence to sleep therapy.

But as the team dug deeper during their time in the Foundry, they uncovered a bigger and more urgent opportunity. Durable Medical Equipment (DME) providers, responsible for supplying and supporting patients with CPAP machines and other critical devices, were stuck between trying to grow while maintaining excellent patient care and adherence to treatment. Staffing shortages, rising costs, and manual support processes left DMEs unable to keep up with patient needs.

item.title: New IQSS Web Site Launched!
item.description:
The Institute for Quantitative Social Science is proud to announce the launch of a completely redesigned website, developed through an extensive cross-team collaboration that brought together expertise from across the organization. The new site reflects IQSS’s evolution as a hub for quantitative research methods, data science innovation, and collaborative scholarship, providing improved access to information about programs, services, and research initiatives.

The redesigned website features enhanced navigation, making it easier for visitors to discover the breadth of resources and support available through IQSS. From data science consultation services and training opportunities to information about affiliated faculty research and major projects like Dataverse, the new site provides clearer pathways to the tools and expertise that support quantitative research across Harvard. The modern design better showcases the innovative work happening at IQSS while maintaining accessibility and usability for diverse audiences.

item.title: New R Package {clarify}
item.description:
Data Science Services at IQSS has released a new R package {clarify} to provide functionality previously provided by the {Zelig} package.

The package “uses simulation to make interpretable inferences about any quantity of interest from a wide variety of statistical models.” It restores and extends functionality that was previously available through the {Zelig} package for R and the Clarify package for Stata. The package supports many model classes from a variety of other R packages and offers methods for computing interpretable quantities from models fit to multiply imputed data.

item.title: Newspaper Endorsements and the Importance of Candidate Quality in Politics
item.description:
Kevin DeLuca (Yale, Harvard PhD 2023, IQSS alumnus) describes political endorsement data he collected as part of his dissertation research, exploring how newspaper endorsements relate to candidate quality and electoral outcomes.

DeLuca’s research assembled a comprehensive dataset of newspaper endorsements across multiple election cycles, enabling systematic analysis of patterns that were previously only studied anecdotally. His findings contribute to understanding the role of institutional media in democratic elections.

The project demonstrates the value of IQSS’s training environment, where graduate students develop both the methodological skills and the data collection persistence needed to produce original datasets that advance political science. DeLuca’s subsequent position at Yale reflects the competitive academic placements that IQSS affiliates regularly achieve.

item.title: Now Announcing the OpenDP Library 0.7
item.description:
The OpenDP Project is excited to announce the release of OpenDP Library 0.7, featuring important architectural additions that expand the framework’s flexibility and power for building differentially private systems. These architectural enhancements provide developers with more sophisticated tools for composing privacy-preserving analyses while maintaining the rigorous mathematical guarantees that are central to OpenDP’s mission.

Version 0.7 introduces new architectural components that enable more complex privacy accounting scenarios and support advanced composition patterns. These additions make it easier to build sophisticated data analysis pipelines that combine multiple differentially private operations while precisely tracking cumulative privacy loss. The enhanced architecture also improves support for interactive analyses and adaptive queries, expanding the range of use cases where differential privacy can be practically deployed without excessive privacy budget consumption.

item.title: On data centers, local vibes matter more than electricity prices
item.description:
A survey led by faculty affiliate Stephen Ansolabehere, the Elting R. Morison Professor of Political Science and Professor of Government at Harvard, explores what Americans prioritize regarding large data centers opening in their communities. The research found that local community concerns matter more to residents than electricity pricing when it comes to data center siting decisions, challenging conventional assumptions about public priorities in infrastructure development.

As demand for data centers surges driven by cloud computing, artificial intelligence, and digital services, companies and policymakers have often focused on electricity costs and power grid capacity as primary factors in location decisions. However, Ansolabehere’s research reveals that community members place greater emphasis on factors such as environmental impacts, effects on local character and quality of life, job creation, property values, and how well the facility integrates with existing community fabric. These “local vibes”—the intangible qualities that define a community’s identity and lived experience—emerge as more salient to residents than the economic considerations that dominate industry planning.

item.title: OpenDP Library 0.6 is Now Available
item.description:
The OpenDP Project is pleased to announce the release of OpenDP Library 0.6, a significant milestone that lays crucial groundwork for incorporating formal mathematical proofs directly into the OpenDP codebase. This release represents an important step toward the project’s long-term vision of providing not just privacy-preserving algorithms, but mathematically verified guarantees that the implementations faithfully represent their theoretical specifications.

Version 0.6 introduces infrastructure that will eventually enable automatic verification of privacy proofs, bridging the gap between theoretical differential privacy and practical implementations. This work addresses a fundamental challenge in privacy-preserving systems: ensuring that code actually provides the privacy guarantees it claims. By building toward machine-checkable proofs, OpenDP aims to provide unprecedented confidence in the privacy properties of deployed systems, reducing the risk of implementation errors that could compromise sensitive data.

item.title: OpenDP Seeks Interviewees for Software Study
item.description:
The OpenDP Project is seeking data analysts, researchers, and data professionals to participate in a software usability study focused on DP Creator, an innovative application designed to make differential privacy more accessible to practitioners without deep technical expertise in privacy mathematics. This research study aims to gather valuable feedback that will shape the development of tools that enable broader adoption of privacy-preserving data analysis methods.

DP Creator represents an important step toward democratizing differential privacy by providing intuitive interfaces for creating differentially private analyses without requiring users to understand the underlying mathematical complexities. The software study will involve testing the application with realistic data analysis tasks, providing feedback on the user interface and workflow, and sharing insights about how differential privacy tools can better serve the needs of working data analysts. Participants’ experiences and suggestions will directly inform ongoing development to make the tool more effective and user-friendly.

item.title: PaCSS 2022: Call for Proposals
item.description: The annual Politics and Computational Social Science (PaCSS) conference has issued its call for proposals for the 2022 meeting, inviting researchers to submit work that advances the intersection of political science and computational methods. This conference brings together scholars using innovative computational approaches to study political phenomena, from social media analysis and text mining to machine learning and network science applied to questions of governance, political behavior, and democratic processes.

item.title: Partisanship and pandemic preparedness in Indonesia
item.description:
Seth Soderborg explores how partisan politics in Indonesia can affect pandemic preparedness decisions, drawing parallels with U.S. experiences during COVID-19. His research examines whether the politicization of public health measures observed in American politics also manifests in other democratic contexts.

The study finds that in Indonesia, the relationship between partisanship and pandemic preparedness depends significantly on local context—particularly the political alignment between local and national leaders. This geographic variation provides leverage for understanding how political incentives can either promote or undermine public health responses.

item.title: Peer Pre-Review is now rapidPeer
item.description:
IQSS has rebranded its Peer Pre Review service as rapidPeer.

The service addresses frustrations with “the speed and quality of your journal peer reviews” by offering free, double-blind peer reviews for research papers, grants, or preregistration plans within 30 days. Reviewers are field-specific experts from leading institutions who provide feedback on both quantitative and qualitative research.

For Harvard researchers, rapidPeer delivers “quick, actionable insights that help refine your work and expedite the publication process,” potentially saving months in the submission cycle. To date, the service has supported 140 scholars in maximizing their publishing success.

item.title: Pope Leo XIV names epidemiology professor to Vatican advisory group
item.description:
Tyler VanderWeele, the John L. Loeb and Frances Lehman Loeb Professor of Epidemiology at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health and director of the Human Flourishing Program at IQSS, has been appointed by Pope Leo XIV to the Pontifical Academy of Social Sciences. This prestigious appointment recognizes VanderWeele’s groundbreaking work on human flourishing, well-being, and the role of faith and virtue in health and society.

The Pontifical Academy of Social Sciences serves as an advisory body to the Vatican on matters related to social, economic, and political issues affecting humanity. VanderWeele joins an international group of distinguished scholars who provide guidance to the Holy See on complex social questions. His expertise in epidemiological methods, causal inference, and the measurement of well-being brings a rigorous quantitative social science perspective to the academy’s deliberations on human development and flourishing.

item.title: Powerful computing in comfortable surroundings: the IQSS Computer Labs
item.description:
by Danielle Benaroche Gottesman

Nestled within Harvard’s Knafel Building—known primarily for government and international studies—is a high-end computing refuge. It’s always open and filled with cutting-edge technology and software.

“Most computer labs have gray walls and feel very plain,” says IQSS Computer Lab Manager Randy Kiessig. “We have a colorful atmosphere, beautiful wood desks, and the winter garden—which feels a lot like a coffee shop.”

Students have long benefited from on-campus computer labs, but pandemic-era lifestyle changes shifted user habits toward personal computer use in isolated spaces. The labs aim to meet the full spectrum of needs for Harvard students, staff, and faculty, offering both pleasing and practical environments for focused work.

item.title: Preparing the Next Steps and the Next Generation: Latanya Sweeney's Data Privacy Lab
item.description:
Latanya Sweeney’s Data Privacy Lab and Public Interest Tech Lab explore digital information risks in today’s technology-filled world. The labs serve dual purposes: conducting cutting-edge research on privacy vulnerabilities while training the next generation of scholars who will grapple with these challenges.

Sweeney’s approach combines technical investigation—actively probing systems for privacy weaknesses—with policy analysis and public engagement. Her labs have exposed re-identification risks in health data, discriminatory patterns in online advertising, and surveillance capabilities hidden in everyday technologies.

item.title: PSR Helps Scholars Navigate Survey Research in Social Distance
item.description:
The Program on Survey Research (PSR), led by Associate Director Chase Harrison, helps a lot of people at Harvard and beyond with survey methodology. When COVID-19 forced a shift from face-to-face to online research, PSR became an essential resource for scholars adapting their methods.

Harrison and his team provided guidance on transitioning in-person surveys to digital formats, addressing challenges around response rates, sample representativeness, and measurement equivalence across modes. Their expertise helped researchers maintain data quality during an unprecedented disruption to social science fieldwork.

item.title: Qualitative Research at IQSS: Numbers and Narratives Combined
item.description:
Qualitative research forms the backbone of our knowledge about human society. Social scientists at IQSS explore crime, corruption, race, culture, violence, and well-being using methods that go beyond numbers to capture the richness of human experience.

While IQSS is known for quantitative methods, the institute recognizes that the deepest understanding of social phenomena often requires combining statistical analysis with qualitative approaches—interviews, ethnography, archival research, and case studies. This integration produces insights that neither approach could achieve alone.

item.title: Register Early for the Dataverse Community Meeting 2026
item.description:
Early bird registration is now open for the Dataverse Community Meeting 2026, taking place May 12-15 in Barcelona, Spain. This year’s meeting is co-organized with the Barcelona Supercomputing Center, bringing together the global Dataverse community in one of Europe’s most vibrant and innovative cities. The event promises to be a landmark gathering for researchers, data managers, repository administrators, and developers working with the Dataverse platform.

The Dataverse Community Meeting serves as the premier annual event for the worldwide network of institutions using Dataverse for research data management and sharing. Attendees will have opportunities to learn about the latest developments in the Dataverse software, share experiences and best practices, participate in hands-on technical workshops, and connect with colleagues from around the globe. The meeting typically features presentations on innovative uses of Dataverse, discussions of data curation challenges, and planning sessions for future platform enhancements.

item.title: Register now for the 2023 OpenDP Community Meeting
item.description:
The OpenDP Project is excited to announce that registration is now open for the 2023 OpenDP Community Meeting, marking the first in-person gathering of the differential privacy community centered around this groundbreaking open-source initiative. This event brings together researchers, developers, data practitioners, and privacy advocates who are advancing the science and practice of differential privacy through the OpenDP ecosystem.

The community meeting will feature technical presentations, hands-on workshops, and collaborative discussions about the latest developments in differential privacy tools and methodologies. Attendees will have the opportunity to learn about new features in the OpenDP Library, share implementation experiences, and contribute to the project’s future direction. Whether you’re already using OpenDP in production systems or exploring how differential privacy can enhance your data protection practices, this gathering offers valuable networking and learning opportunities.

item.title: Registration is open for Harvard DataFest 2021
item.description:
Harvard’s Institute for Quantitative Social Science is excited to announce that registration is now open for Harvard DataFest 2021, an intensive bootcamp designed to help researchers develop, refine, and advance their research projects through hands-on learning and collaborative problem-solving. This immersive event brings together graduate students, postdoctoral researchers, and faculty members working across the social sciences to tackle methodological challenges and accelerate their scholarly work.

DataFest provides participants with concentrated time to work on their research while accessing expert guidance on quantitative methods, computational tools, and data management strategies. The bootcamp format combines structured workshops on specific technical topics with dedicated project time where attendees can apply new skills to their own research questions. Experienced instructors and peer participants create a collaborative learning environment where researchers can troubleshoot challenges, discover new approaches, and make meaningful progress on projects that might otherwise stall due to technical obstacles.

item.title: Registration is Open for the Winter 2022 GIS Institute
item.description:
Harvard’s Center for Geographic Analysis is pleased to announce that registration is now open for the Winter 2022 GIS Institute, a comprehensive two-week intensive training course that provides participants with foundational and advanced skills in geographic information systems and spatial analysis. This immersive program is designed for graduate students, postdoctoral researchers, faculty members, and professionals who want to incorporate spatial analysis into their research or professional practice.

The GIS Institute offers structured, hands-on instruction covering essential GIS concepts, software tools, and analytical techniques. Participants learn to work with spatial data, create compelling maps, perform sophisticated geographic analyses, and integrate GIS methods into research workflows. The intensive format allows for deep engagement with the material, progressing from fundamental concepts to advanced applications over the two-week period. Expert instructors from the Center for Geographic Analysis provide personalized guidance, helping participants overcome learning challenges and connect GIS methods to their specific research questions.

item.title: Registration Open for the 8th Annual Dataverse Community Meeting
item.description:
The Dataverse Project is pleased to announce that registration is now open for the 8th Annual Dataverse Community Meeting, bringing together the global network of researchers, repository administrators, developers, and data professionals who make up the vibrant Dataverse ecosystem. This year’s meeting offers a valuable opportunity to connect with peers, learn about the latest platform developments, and contribute to shaping the future of research data management infrastructure.

The 2022 meeting will feature a rich program of presentations, workshops, and discussions covering diverse aspects of the Dataverse platform and research data management more broadly. Attendees can expect sessions on new software features, case studies from institutions implementing Dataverse in innovative ways, technical deep dives for developers, and strategic discussions about data curation, preservation, and open science policy. The meeting provides crucial face-to-face time for collaboration and networking that strengthens the community bonds essential to successful open-source development.

item.title: Remembering Sidney Verba
item.description:
A tribute to Sidney Verba of Harvard, who passed on March 4, 2019. Verba was a towering figure in political science whose work on political participation, civic engagement, and democratic theory influenced generations of scholars. He was also a founding influence on what would become IQSS.

Verba’s research, particularly his landmark study “Voice and Equality” (with Kay Lehman Schlozman and Henry Brady), established the modern framework for understanding why some citizens participate in politics while others do not. His emphasis on rigorous empirical measurement of democratic engagement exemplified the quantitative approach that IQSS champions.

item.title: Restructuring the Dataverse UI
item.description:
The Dataverse Project has announced ambitious plans to fundamentally restructure the platform’s user interface, transforming it into an API-first architecture that will provide greater flexibility, improved performance, and better support for diverse use cases. This significant architectural evolution represents a major investment in the long-term sustainability and extensibility of the Dataverse platform, positioning it to meet the evolving needs of research data repositories for years to come.

The move to an API-first architecture involves separating the user interface from the backend data and business logic, creating clean interfaces that allow for multiple front-end implementations. This approach enables institutions to customize their Dataverse interfaces more extensively while still benefiting from core platform capabilities. It also facilitates the development of specialized interfaces for specific communities or workflows, mobile applications, and integration with other tools and systems. The architectural change will make Dataverse more adaptable to different institutional contexts and research domain requirements.

item.title: Revolutionizing Research through AI-Powered Data Sharing and Curation
item.description:
TurboCurator by ICPSR is an interactive tool designed to streamline research data deposit at Dataverse, developed through intensive collaboration between institutions. The tool uses artificial intelligence to automate many of the time-consuming steps in preparing research data for sharing.

Traditionally, depositing data in a repository requires researchers to manually enter metadata, organize files, and ensure compliance with standards—a process that can take hours and discourages many from sharing their data at all. TurboCurator uses AI to suggest metadata, identify data types, and guide researchers through the curation process.

item.title: Ryan Enos explores partisan segregation
item.description:
Professor of Government Ryan Enos has spent much of his career exploring how local geographic context shapes political behavior and partisan segregation. His research reveals that where people live—and who their neighbors are—profoundly influences their political attitudes and behaviors.

Enos’s work demonstrates that partisan segregation is self-reinforcing: as communities become more politically homogeneous, residents develop more extreme views and lose the ability to understand opposing perspectives. This finding has important implications for democratic governance and social cohesion.

item.title: Save the Date: 2023 Dataverse Community Meeting
item.description:
The Dataverse Project invites the global research data management community to save the date for the 9th Annual Dataverse Community Meeting, taking place in the historic and vibrant city of Braga, Portugal. This highly anticipated gathering brings together repository administrators, developers, data professionals, and researchers from institutions worldwide to share experiences, discuss best practices, and shape the future of the Dataverse platform.

The meeting in Braga will feature technical presentations, workshops, and collaborative sessions covering the latest developments in Dataverse software, emerging trends in research data management, and strategies for supporting open science initiatives. Attendees will have opportunities to learn from peers managing Dataverse installations across diverse institutional contexts, from small university repositories to large international collaborations. The European location makes this an especially valuable opportunity for institutions across Europe, Africa, and the Middle East to participate in the Dataverse community.

item.title: Science needs contrarians, and contrarians need support
item.description:
The Extraordinary Claims, Extraordinary Evidence (ECEE) program at IQSS supports Harvard faculty undertaking high-risk research that could provide novel insights by testing dominant views in social science. The program provides tailored support including data expertise, infrastructure, and funding.

ECEE recognizes that scientific progress often comes from researchers willing to challenge established consensus—but that such work faces unique obstacles in traditional funding and publication systems. By providing a confidential environment with dedicated resources, the program enables faculty to pursue ideas they might otherwise set aside.

item.title: Streamlining the Publication Process with Magaro Peer Pre-Review
item.description:
Stephen Chaudoin laments that “you only get so many bites of the apple with journals”—the traditional publication process is lengthy, uncertain, and offers limited opportunities for constructive feedback before formal review. The Magaro Peer Pre-Review Program addresses this gap.

The program, funded by a generous gift from Alexander and Diviya Magaro, provides researchers with professional-quality peer reviews before they submit to journals. Reviewers read full papers and offer detailed, constructive suggestions, helping authors strengthen their work and avoid common pitfalls that lead to rejection.

item.title: The art of the online interface
item.description:
By Colleen Walsh

New desktop application streamlines connection between IQSS’s Dataverse and Sid

A new application called LOOP, developed at Harvard’s Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS), aims to simplify the workflow for researchers who want to analyze data from the Harvard Dataverse using Sid—Harvard’s version of the open-source Open OnDemand dashboard that connects scholars with supercomputers.

Len Wisniewski, senior director of research computing at IQSS, leads the Sid team that co-designed the LOOP application with the Dataverse team. The software targets researchers across disciplines—from political science to psychology to the physical sciences.

item.title: The Community that Makes the Center: the Whiteboards of IQSS
item.description:
by Gary King

IQSS director Gary King reflects on the origins of the writing surfaces that cover the hallways of the Institute, as featured in a recent article by the Harvard Gazette.

When King started IQSS, he interviewed many current and former Harvard deans and presidents to understand what made the best centers succeed. He looked at places like the legendary Cavendish in London, Bell Labs, ISR, and others. Everyone he spoke with had a different story but arrived at the same conclusion. One person would emphasize great space, but then acknowledge that some great centers lacked great space. Others would stress funding, then concede it wasn’t really about money. But ultimately, every single person he consulted landed on the same answer: community. As King writes, “It is the intellectual community that makes the center.” He had a specific notion of how to build that community at IQSS, described in an article he wrote about IQSS, and notes that “this method wound up working great!”

item.title: The Enigma of the Undecided Voter
item.description:
by Vinay Mehra

Originally posted on Vinay Mehra’s Substack.

The article explores the conventional image of the undecided voter—the suburban mom or blue-collar worker who can’t choose between candidates—and argues this framing is far too narrow. Researchers at Harvard’s Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS) suggest that the firm distinction between “decided” and “undecided” voters may not actually exist.

The Real Swing

The article contends that so-called undecided voters aren’t a separate breed standing on a fence. Rather, they sit near the middle of the political spectrum, close to a threshold where political shifts make their vote changes more visible. The entire electorate sways together; those in the middle simply show the movement most clearly. Even solidly partisan areas experience shifts—just less obviously.

item.title: Tracking the American electorate: the Cooperative Election Study
item.description:
The Cooperative Election Study, supported by IQSS, offers key insights into the minds of American voters. Running since 2006, the study surveys tens of thousands of Americans before and after each election, providing unprecedented detail about electoral behavior at the local level.

The study’s massive sample size—typically 50,000 or more respondents—enables analysis at the congressional district level, something impossible with standard national polls. This granularity has made the CES an essential resource for researchers studying representation, accountability, and geographic variation in political attitudes.

item.title: Travel Time Estimation for Geospatial Big Data
item.description:
Researchers at the Center for Geographic Analysis explore drive time estimation’s importance in urban planning, transportation, public health, and healthcare accessibility. Their case study examines travel times to healthcare facilities across the United States, revealing geographic disparities in access.

The research addresses a fundamental challenge in geospatial analysis: accurately estimating travel times for millions of origin-destination pairs requires enormous computational resources and sophisticated algorithms that account for road networks, traffic patterns, and geographic barriers.

item.title: Turns out two-parent households are no fix for racial inequality
item.description: IQSS affiliate Christina Cross, Associate Professor of Sociology at Harvard University, has published a book that critically examines one of the most persistent claims in debates about racial inequality: that promoting two-parent nuclear families is a simple solution to closing racial gaps in economic and social outcomes. Through rigorous analysis of demographic and economic data, Cross challenges this conventional wisdom and reveals the limitations of family structure as an explanation for racial disparities.

item.title: Twitter Sentiment Geographical Index
item.description:
The Center for Geographic Analysis at Harvard’s IQSS explores how Twitter evolved from a simple social networking platform into a powerful tool for sentiment analysis and geographic understanding of public opinion. The development of the Twitter Sentiment Geographical Index represents an innovative application of social media data to track emotional patterns and attitudes across geographic regions, providing researchers with unprecedented insight into spatial variations in public sentiment.

By combining natural language processing techniques to analyze the emotional content of tweets with geolocation data, researchers can map sentiment patterns across cities, regions, and countries. This approach enables studies of how attitudes vary geographically and change over time in response to events, policies, or social trends. The Twitter Sentiment Geographical Index has applications across diverse research domains, from understanding political attitudes and election dynamics to tracking public health concerns and responses to natural disasters.

item.title: Update on IQSS and Microsoft OpenDP Collaboration
item.description:
IQSS and Microsoft provided an update on their collaboration to develop open-source tools for differential privacy, enabling researchers to discover insights while protecting data. The partnership combines Harvard’s theoretical expertise with Microsoft’s engineering resources.

The update details progress on the OpenDP platform, which provides verified privacy guarantees for data analysis. The collaboration has produced both a software library (the OpenDP Library) and research advancing the theoretical foundations of privacy-preserving computation.

item.title: Using Data to Map the Political Legacy of Slavery
item.description:
Matthew Blackwell and Maya Sen’s book “Deep Roots” won APSA’s 2019 William H. Riker Book Award for the best book on political economy published during the past three calendar years. The book traces how slavery’s institutional legacy continues to shape American politics today.

Using a combination of historical data, geographic analysis, and modern survey evidence, Blackwell and Sen demonstrate that Southern counties with higher historical concentrations of slavery exhibit distinctive political attitudes and behaviors that persist into the present—even after controlling for contemporary demographic and economic factors.

item.title: Visiting Seoul National University for Dataverse
item.description:
Philip Durbin recounts a visit by five colleagues to Seoul National University for Dataverse talks and training, hosted by Professor Wonsuk Lee. The trip reflects the Dataverse Project’s growing international presence and commitment to supporting installations worldwide.

The visit included technical training sessions on Dataverse administration, presentations on best practices for research data management, and discussions about how to adapt the platform for Korean research communities. Such visits help ensure that international Dataverse installations receive the support they need to serve their local research communities effectively.

item.title: Waves 1 and 2 of the Global Flourishing Study Are Now Open to the Public
item.description: The Human Flourishing Program at Harvard University has made a landmark contribution to social science research with the public release of both Wave 1 and Wave 2 data from the Global Flourishing Study. This massive dataset includes responses from roughly 200,000 participants across 22 countries, making it one of the most comprehensive longitudinal studies of human well-being ever conducted. The data covers six fundamental domains of flourishing: happiness and life satisfaction, physical and mental health, meaning and purpose, character and virtue, close social relationships, and financial and material stability.

item.title: What science says about Moms happiness advice
item.description:
IQSS affiliate Daniel Gilbert, the Edgar Pierce Professor of Psychology at Harvard University, delivered a compelling lecture exploring the intersection of scientific research and timeless maternal wisdom about happiness. Gilbert, known for his groundbreaking work on affective forecasting and happiness research, shared insights from decades of psychological studies that validate many of the common-sense principles our mothers have been telling us for years.

During the lecture, Gilbert explained how rigorous scientific investigation into human happiness has repeatedly confirmed conventional wisdom about what makes people truly happy. From the importance of gratitude and social connections to the value of helping others and maintaining perspective during difficult times, research data consistently supports the age-old advice that mothers have been dispensing for generations. Gilbert’s presentation highlighted how psychological science has progressed from questioning these intuitive beliefs to understanding the mechanisms that make them effective.

item.title: What's Next for This Year's Graduating IQSS Affiliates
item.description:
The IQSS community includes affiliated graduate students across Harvard University engaged in a wide range of social science research. Each year, some graduate students depart as they complete their programs. Below are updates from graduating affiliates moving on to continue their work beyond Harvard.

Stephanie Kestelman (PhD, Economics)

Stephanie is remaining at Harvard for a postdoctoral fellowship, working with Professor Rebecca Diamond to evaluate potential impacts of Cambridge’s new zoning ordinance.

item.title: Where to Next? Outgoing IQSS graduate affiliates share plans
item.description:
IQSS staff piece about departing graduate student affiliates from programs across Harvard and their future plans. Each year, the end of the academic year brings the departure of affiliated graduate students who have been active in social science research.

The departing affiliates head to positions across academia, policy organizations, and the private sector, carrying with them the quantitative training and collaborative research experience they gained at IQSS. Their diverse career paths—from assistant professorships to research positions at think tanks to roles in tech companies—reflect the breadth of opportunities available to quantitative social scientists.

item.title: Where to now? Outgoing IQSS graduate affiliates share their next steps
item.description:
Profiles of departing graduate students from the IQSS community cohort as they share plans after the academic year ends. The 2023 graduating class represents the diversity of disciplines and methodological approaches that characterize the IQSS affiliate community.

Graduating affiliates are heading to faculty positions, postdoctoral fellowships, and research roles at institutions across the country and around the world. Their research spans political science, economics, sociology, public policy, and data science—united by a commitment to rigorous quantitative methods.

item.title: Why self-appraisals may not be best way to judge job performance
item.description:
Faculty affiliate Iris Bohnet, Albert Pratt Professor of Business and Government at Harvard Kennedy School and director of the Women and Public Policy Program, has published new research challenging the widespread practice of using self-appraisals in workplace performance evaluations. Her recent paper examines how worker performance ratings are affected by various biases and structural factors that can undermine the accuracy and fairness of self-assessment processes.

Bohnet’s research reveals that self-appraisals, while seemingly empowering to employees, often introduce systematic distortions into performance evaluation systems. The study found that self-ratings are influenced by factors including individual confidence levels, cultural backgrounds, and gender, with some groups systematically underrating their performance while others tend toward inflation. These patterns can inadvertently perpetuate workplace inequalities, as more modest self-assessors may be disadvantaged in compensation and promotion decisions even when their actual performance is strong.
